summary: This advice column covers four reader questions spanning philosophy of mathematics, work-life balance, academic economics research, and psychology of criticism. Cathy O'Neil argues that mathematics is a tool of approximation rather than a literal description of reality, advises a bored working mother to demand more from both her career and her husband, critiques a paper on Fields Medalists' productivity for questionable statistical methodology and cherry-picked comparison groups, and explains how psychological sensitivities create selection biases in how we perceive others' flaws.
key_points: Mathematics doesn't describe the universe literally - it's an approximation tool, and idealized concepts like 'circles' don't exist in physical reality
Being bored at work is unacceptable self-neglect; mothers should model self-advocacy and career fulfillment for their children
Economists shouldn't be trusted to run the economy given their vested interests, and their statistical methodology is often questionable
The Fields Medal productivity study appears to cherry-pick comparison groups by requiring 'above-median citations' in ways that muddy the analysis
Psychological sensitivities create selection bias - we notice in others what we're insecure about in ourselves
money_quotes: As a mathematician I'm here to tell you that mathematics doesn't describe the universe. It's at most used as a tool to understand certain parts of the universe, but only at the level of an approximation.

There's no such thing as a circle in reality. It's an idealized shape we use in mathematics that comes in super handy for various reasons, but because actual matter is made up of stuff, there's never going to be a true circle, except in our brains.
You need to be interested in your own life, and being bored off your butt is seriously not cutting it. Be more selfish than that, and do it for your kids.
He's not helpful because he's gotten away with not being helpful. It's a deal you made with him, possibly (probably) without thinking enough about it. Time to renegotiate.
Without even reading the paper I'd say we shouldn't trust economists to run our economy. They have already proved their vested interests are too distracting for such a responsibility.
If you were doing stuff in order to win a Fields Medal, then after getting it, you'd stop, right, and do something else that's interesting?
When someone is sensitized to an issue, even if it's not one of insecurity, then they see it everywhere, all the time, as if for the first time.
key_insight: Mathematical models are approximations rather than reality, and similarly, the frameworks we use to understand careers, relationships, and research all deserve skeptical examination of their underlying assumptions.
